chunk_summary: This section outlines anthropometry-based malnutrition classification and referral thresholds for infants under 2 years, emphasizing accurate measurements, z-score interpretation, and urgent referral criteria for severe cases.
learning_objectives: Understand the use of anthropometry as a clinical decision tool for malnutrition classification in children under 2 years.
Learn the interpretation of WFA, LFA, and WFL z-scores and their thresholds for stunting, wasting, and underweight.
Identify urgent referral criteria for severe wasting, nutritional edema, and feeding difficulties in infants under 6 months.
key_facts: Wasting is defined as WFL z-score ≤ -2 (moderate: -2 to -3; severe: < -3).
Stunting is defined as LFA z-score ≤ -2.
Bilateral pitting edema indicates nutritional edema and requires SAM treatment regardless of measurements.
MUAC cutoffs are validated for ages 6–59 months and should not replace WFL assessment in younger infants.
Infants under 6 months with weight loss >10% after day 3–5 or failure to regain birth weight by 10–14 days require escalation.
reference_quote: Use anthropometry as a decision tool, not just documentation. Routinely interpret three indices: weight-for-age (WFA), length-for-age (LFA), and weight-for-length (WFL).
document_key_topics: Feeding difficulties in infants under 6 months
